def: Neurodevelopmental disorder of inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity impairing function; stimulants are first-line and most effective.
A sudden surge of intense fear peaking within minutes, with palpitations, shortness of breath, chest tightness, and a sense of doom.
Chronic, excessive, hard-to-control worry across many areas, with restlessness, fatigue, poor concentration, and muscle tension.
Marked, persistent fear of scrutiny in social or performance situations, leading to avoidance and distress.
Intrusive, unwanted obsessions plus repetitive compulsions done to reduce distress; first-line treatment is SSRIs and ERP.
Persistent re-experiencing, avoidance, hyperarousal, and negative mood after a traumatic event.
Two-plus weeks of low mood or anhedonia with changes in sleep, appetite, energy, concentration, and feelings of worthlessness or guilt.
Mood disorder alternating depression with mania or hypomania; managed with mood stabilizers and antimanic agents, not antidepressants alone.
A sustained elevated or irritable, high-energy state with reduced sleep need, rapid speech and thought, grandiosity, and risky behaviour.
A milder high than mania — elevated, energised mood with reduced sleep need, without marked impairment or psychosis.
Loss of contact with reality — hallucinations, delusions, or disorganised thinking.
Chronic psychotic illness spanning positive (hallucinations, delusions), negative, and cognitive symptom domains; treated with antipsychotics.
Hallucinations and delusions in Parkinson disease; treated with low-dose clozapine or pimavanserin without worsening motor symptoms.
Acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and awareness from a medical cause; often reversible once the trigger is treated.
Acquired, usually progressive decline in memory and other cognitive domains severe enough to impair everyday independence.

aliases: behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia
def: Behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia — agitation, psychosis, mood and sleep disturbance — that drive caregiver burden.
A state of motor restlessness, heightened arousal, and potential aggression needing rapid calming.
Problematic alcohol use with craving, loss of control, tolerance, and withdrawal on stopping.
Autonomic hyperactivity, tremor, anxiety, and seizures on stopping heavy alcohol; the severe form, delirium tremens, is life-threatening.
Compulsive opioid use with tolerance, withdrawal, and craving; treated first-line with opioid agonist therapy.
Opioid toxicity causing unresponsiveness, slow or absent breathing, and pinpoint pupils; reversed with naloxone.
Uncomfortable but rarely dangerous syndrome — anxiety, sweating, GI upset, myalgia, dilated pupils — on stopping opioids.
Nicotine dependence with craving and withdrawal; varenicline is the single most effective cessation agent.
Difficulty falling or staying asleep with daytime impairment despite adequate opportunity to sleep.
A depressive episode arising during pregnancy or after delivery; neurosteroids brexanolone and zuranolone are specific treatments.
A psychiatric emergency days after delivery — rapid-onset confusion, mood lability, and delusions, often bipolar-spectrum, with risk to mother or infant.
A psychomotor syndrome of immobility, mutism, posturing, or excitement; responds to benzodiazepines and ECT.
A childhood-onset neurodevelopmental disorder with chronic motor and vocal tics.
Fear and avoidance of situations where escape is hard or help unavailable — crowds, transit, or being alone.
A prolonged or repeated seizure without recovery between — a neurological emergency; IV or IM benzodiazepines are first-line.
An eating disorder of binge eating with compensatory purging; bupropion is contraindicated because of seizure risk.
Involuntary bedwetting; nocturnal enuresis in children is a classic imipramine indication.
Burning, shooting, or tingling pain from nerve damage; treated with duloxetine, TCAs, and gabapentinoids.
A chronic disorder of excessive daytime sleepiness and sleep attacks; modafinil promotes wakefulness.
Sudden-onset OCD or tics in a child following streptococcal infection (PANDAS/PANS).
Excess serotonergic activity causing agitation, clonus, hyperreflexia, hyperthermia, and autonomic instability — often from drug combinations.
Rare reaction to dopamine blockers: high fever, lead-pipe rigidity, altered consciousness, autonomic instability, and markedly raised CK.
Drug-induced movement effects from dopamine blockade — acute dystonia, akathisia, and parkinsonism.
A distressing inner restlessness with an urge to move; a common antipsychotic effect treated first-line with propranolol.
Sudden sustained muscle spasm of the neck, jaw, tongue, or eyes soon after a dopamine blocker; treat with benztropine or diphenhydramine.
A dystonic reaction with sustained upward or lateral deviation of the eyes.
Antipsychotic-induced tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia mimicking Parkinson disease, caused by dopamine blockade.
Late-onset, often irreversible involuntary movements (classically lip-smacking, tongue, grimacing) after prolonged dopamine blockade.
Antimuscarinic excess: dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, urinary retention, flushed dry skin, hyperthermia, and confusion.
A dangerous blood-pressure surge when tyramine-rich foods are eaten on an irreversible MAOI — the 'cheese reaction'.
Rising lithium levels causing coarse tremor, ataxia, slurred speech, vomiting, confusion, and seizures; a medical emergency.
Antipsychotic-associated weight gain, dyslipidemia, and impaired glucose that raise cardiovascular and diabetes risk.
Flu-like malaise, dizziness, and electric-shock 'brain zaps' after abruptly stopping a short-half-life antidepressant.
Loss of the ability to feel pleasure or interest in normally enjoyable activities — a core depressive symptom.
Inflated self-esteem or belief in special powers or importance — a core manic symptom.
Profuse sweating; a feature of serotonin syndrome, withdrawal, and stimulant toxicity (helps distinguish from anticholinergic dry skin).
Pupillary dilation; seen in serotonergic and anticholinergic toxicity and stimulant intoxication.
Pupillary constriction ('pinpoint pupils') — a hallmark of opioid toxicity.
Exaggerated deep-tendon reflexes; a key feature of serotonin syndrome, contrasting with the hyporeflexia of NMS.
Rhythmic involuntary muscle jerks on sudden stretch; a defining sign of serotonin toxicity.
Increased muscle tone; uniform 'lead-pipe' rigidity is a hallmark of neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Rhythmic involuntary shaking; a fine tremor is common on lithium and valproate, and a coarse tremor flags lithium toxicity.
Impaired coordination and unsteady gait; a sign of lithium toxicity and anticonvulsant excess.
Slurred, poorly articulated speech; seen in lithium toxicity and sedative intoxication.
A drop in blood pressure on standing causing dizziness or faintness, often from alpha-1 blockade.
Elevated prolactin from dopamine blockade, causing breast milk, missed periods, and sexual dysfunction.
A prolonged, often painful erection unrelated to arousal; a urologic emergency classically linked to trazodone via alpha-1 blockade.
Lengthening of the ECG QT interval that raises the risk of the ventricular arrhythmia torsades de pointes.
A polymorphic ventricular tachycardia triggered by QT prolongation; can be fatal.
A severe, dangerous fall in neutrophils leaving the body prone to infection; a clozapine and carbamazepine risk.
Bone-marrow failure producing low red cells, white cells, and platelets; a rare carbamazepine risk.
A low neutrophil count raising infection risk; monitored closely with clozapine.
Low blood sodium that can cause confusion and seizures; linked to carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, and SSRIs.
Excess antidiuretic hormone causing water retention and dilutional hyponatremia; a cause of drug-induced low sodium.
Inflammation of the heart muscle; an early clozapine risk (weeks 1-8) with tachycardia, chest pain, fever, and rising troponin.
Breakdown of muscle releasing myoglobin that can injure the kidneys; a complication of NMS.
A severe, blistering drug reaction of skin and mucous membranes; linked to lamotrigine and carbamazepine.
Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms — rash, fever, and internal organ involvement.
Deep tissue swelling of the lips, tongue, or airway that can be life-threatening.
A mottled, net-like purplish skin discoloration; a signature effect of amantadine.
Confusion and lethargy from high blood ammonia; a valproate complication that can occur even at normal drug levels.
A surge in blood pressure after abruptly stopping an alpha-2 agonist such as clonidine; taper to prevent it.
Marked drop in blood pressure after the first dose of an alpha-1 blocker like prazosin; start low and dose at bedtime.
Impaired desire, arousal, or orgasm; a common SSRI and SNRI effect that bupropion and mirtazapine largely spare.
Memory loss; benzodiazepine hypnotics can cause anterograde amnesia for events while the drug is active.
Slowed, shallow breathing that can be fatal; risk rises sharply when opioids combine with benzodiazepines or alcohol.
Kidney resistance to antidiuretic hormone causing large volumes of dilute urine and thirst; a chronic lithium effect.
Irregular, flowing involuntary movements; VMAT2 inhibitors treat chorea in Huntington disease and tardive dyskinesia.
The brain's susceptibility to seizures; drugs like bupropion, clozapine, and TCAs lower it, raising seizure risk.
The time for a drug's blood level to fall by half; short half-lives worsen withdrawal, long ones cause accumulation.
Gradually adjusting a dose up or down to balance therapeutic effect against tolerability.
An inactive compound the body converts into the active drug (e.g., lisdexamfetamine, clorazepate).
A breakdown product of a drug that is itself pharmacologically active and can prolong or add to its effects.
Extensive breakdown of an oral drug by gut and liver before it reaches the circulation, lowering bioavailability.
A liver enzyme metabolizing many psychotropics; inhibitors like fluoxetine, paroxetine, and bupropion raise substrate levels.
A major liver and gut enzyme metabolizing many drugs; inducers cut levels, including reducing oral-contraceptive efficacy.
A liver enzyme; inhibition (fluvoxamine) or induction (smoking) markedly changes levels of substrates like clozapine and olanzapine.
A liver enzyme metabolizing citalopram and moclobemide; poor metabolizers or inhibitors push levels up.
The point where drug intake equals elimination and blood levels plateau, usually reached after about five half-lives.
The margin between an effective dose and a toxic one; a narrow index (lithium) means small changes matter.
A drug-free interval before starting an interacting agent — e.g., 14 days around an MAOI, up to 5 weeks after fluoxetine.
A large initial dose that reaches therapeutic levels quickly, as with valproate loaded for acute mania.
A drug level drawn just before the next dose (its lowest point); used to monitor lithium and other narrow-index drugs.
A drug that partly activates a receptor — calming excess signalling while providing some when it is low (e.g., aripiprazole, buprenorphine).
A drug that binds a receptor away from its main site to boost the response to its natural signal, as benzodiazepines do at GABA-A.
A drug that speeds up a metabolic enzyme, lowering levels of drugs it clears (e.g., carbamazepine induces CYP3A4).
A drug that blocks a metabolic enzyme, raising levels of drugs it clears — a common source of interactions.
When a drug speeds up its own metabolism over the first weeks, so its level falls and must be rechecked (carbamazepine).
Someone whose genetic enzyme variant clears a drug slowly, so standard doses produce higher-than-expected levels.
A depot antipsychotic injected every 2 weeks to 3 months to maintain steady levels and support adherence.
A dose above which more drug adds no extra effect; partial agonists like buprenorphine have one, barbiturates do not.
A drug that binds a receptor and drives the opposite of an agonist's effect, lowering baseline activity (e.g., pimavanserin at 5-HT2A).
The strongest safety warning on a drug label, flagging serious or life-threatening risks.
Predictable dosing where blood levels rise in proportion to the dose (e.g., pregabalin), unlike gabapentin's saturable absorption.
A liver conjugation pathway with no CYP step that clears the 'LOT' benzos, so it is preserved in liver disease.
One of two mirror-image forms of a molecule; the purified active form may be sold alone (e.g., escitalopram from citalopram).
Gradually lowering one drug while raising its replacement to switch agents safely.
The brain's main inhibitory receptor — a chloride channel that benzodiazepines and barbiturates enhance to calm neural activity.
A drug that depletes presynaptic dopamine by blocking its vesicular transporter; treats tardive dyskinesia and chorea.
A drug that blocks the glutamate NMDA receptor; memantine uses this for dementia, amantadine for parkinsonism.
A drug that blocks acetylcholine breakdown to boost cholinergic signalling; used symptomatically in Alzheimer dementia.
A steroid that modulates brain receptors; GABA-A neurosteroids brexanolone and zuranolone treat postpartum depression.
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor — first-line antidepressants for depression and anxiety with a favourable safety profile.
Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (e.g., venlafaxine, duloxetine); treats depression, anxiety, and some pain.
Monoamine oxidase inhibitor — a potent but interaction-prone antidepressant class needing a tyramine diet and drug washouts.
An older antidepressant class that is effective but anticholinergic and cardiotoxic in overdose via sodium-channel blockade.
Norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor (bupropion) — activating and sparing of weight and sexual side effects.
Noradrenergic and specific serotonergic antidepressant (mirtazapine) — sedating and appetite-stimulating.
GABA-A enhancers used for anxiety, insomnia, seizures, and withdrawal; effective but habit-forming with tolerance.
Non-benzodiazepine hypnotics (zopiclone, zolpidem) acting at the benzo GABA-A site, used short-term for insomnia.
An older sedative that prolongs GABA-A channel opening with no ceiling effect — dangerous and lethal in overdose (e.g., phenobarbital).
Typical antipsychotic — a potent dopamine D2 blocker with prominent EPS risk (e.g., haloperidol).
Atypical antipsychotic — blocks D2 and 5-HT2A with generally less EPS but more metabolic risk.
A typical antipsychotic class (e.g., chlorpromazine, fluphenazine) where potency drives the EPS-versus-sedation trade-off.
A high-potency typical antipsychotic class (haloperidol, droperidol) with strong D2 blockade and EPS and QT risk.
A typical antipsychotic class (e.g., zuclopenthixol, flupenthixol) often used in depot form.
Dopamine/norepinephrine-boosting agents (methylphenidate, amphetamines) — first-line, most effective ADHD treatment; controlled substances.
Dual orexin receptor antagonist (lemborexant) — blocks the brain's wake signal to promote sleep without dependence.
A selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (atomoxetine); a non-controlled ADHD option with delayed 2-4 week onset.
Agents (clonidine, guanfacine, dexmedetomidine) that lower sympathetic outflow; used in ADHD, agitation, and withdrawal.
An alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist (prazosin) that lowers blood pressure and is used for PTSD nightmares.
A beta-adrenergic antagonist (propranolol); first-line for akathisia and useful for tremor and performance anxiety.
A drug that prevents manic and depressive relapse in bipolar disorder (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine).
Maintenance buprenorphine or methadone that prevents withdrawal and craving in opioid use disorder.
A reversible MAO-A inhibitor (moclobemide) that tyramine can displace, so no strict diet — but serotonin cautions remain.
A genetic marker; carriers (more common in some Asian ancestries) have high SJS/TEN risk with carbamazepine — screen before starting.
A muscle enzyme; a markedly raised CK signals muscle breakdown, as in neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
The count of infection-fighting neutrophils; monitored on clozapine to catch agranulocytosis early.
Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale — a standardized exam tracking tardive dyskinesia over time.
A structured scale rating alcohol-withdrawal severity to guide symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing.
A list of medications best avoided in older adults due to high risk, such as strongly anticholinergic drugs.
